purpose: Black-box smoke scenarios for checking whether model CLIs discover and follow Agentic Workspace startup, planning, and closeout paths.

prompt_transport_guidance.large_prompt_rule: Adapters used for long-horizon evaluator prompts must avoid embedding large prompts directly in argv on Windows. Prefer prompt_transport with file_args when the CLI supports attachments; for plain codex use file-reference prompt_transport so argv carries only the prompt-file path.
prompt_transport_guidance.codex_sbx_status: codex-sbx uses prompt-file metadata but currently fails early with an actionable unsupported error if the rendered Windows command would exceed the command-line limit.

prompt_transport.file_prompt: Read the attached prompt file and follow its instructions exactly. Treat it as the complete task prompt.
Read the complete prompt from this file and follow it exactly: {prompt_file}

scenarios.prompt: You are acting as a coding agent in this repository. Start the way the repo instructs a new agent to start. Do not implement anything yet. Report the first repo instruction or command you used, the next safe action, and any ambiguity. Keep the final answer concise.
expected_signals: reads AGENTS.md or .agentic-workspace/WORKFLOW.md
runs or recommends agentic-workspace start before deeper startup commands
does not invent planning files
score_notes: Good: CLI-first orientation before broad reads.
Bad: starts editing code or writes state.toml without package commands.

scenarios.prompt: Make a tiny wording-only README improvement in this repo: change the first sentence so it says this fixture is for external model CLI dogfooding. Keep setup overhead proportional to this direct edit. Final answer should name the file changed and the proof command, if any.
expected_signals: uses startup guidance lightly
edits only README.md
does not create lane, epic, execplan, or decomposition artifacts for a direct wording edit
uses minimal proof such as inspecting the diff
score_notes: Good: minimal startup, one scoped edit, narrow proof.
Bad: creates planning state or broad handoff artifacts for a trivial edit.

scenarios.prompt: You are about to make a narrow README edit, but do not edit files yet. Use the repo's smallest command output that can tell you the next implementation decision for README.md. Report the exact command used, the next action, and the validation command list. Avoid reading raw .agentic-workspace files unless the command output tells you to.
expected_signals: uses implement --changed rather than full diagnostics
identifies the next action from command output
reports validation commands without dumping full proof detail
does not read raw workspace files first
score_notes: Good: one small command answer, no edits, concise next action.
Bad: reads raw workspace files, runs full diagnostics first, or cannot identify the next step.

prompt_variants.prompt: Build a small ecommerce application in this repo. This is broad work that may span multiple implementation slices. Prepare the repo so a later agent can safely continue from a bounded first slice, but do not build the app yet. Final answer should name the files or commands used.

I want a small ecommerce app here, but this session should only leave the repo ready for a future coding pass. Shape the durable continuation state for a bounded first slice without scaffolding the app itself. Final answer should name the repo-visible state you left behind.

expected_signals: classifies work as lane/epic-shaped before implementation
uses schema-backed planning or decomposition surfaces
does not create freehand epic markdown
score_notes: Good: uses agentic-planning new-plan or schema-backed decomposition template.
Bad: creates generic PM-shaped JSON or ecommerce-epic.md with late-stage task detail.

scenarios.prompt: You need to prepare for broad work in this repo, but you are not sure which repo-supported preparation commands exist. Use local repo instructions and command help before naming any lifecycle command. If your runtime has its own plan or todo tools, treat them as private working memory. Do not create or edit files. Final answer should list the exact commands you verified and the command you would use next.
expected_signals: runs or reads agentic-workspace --help and agentic-workspace planning --help
names only commands that exist in the current CLI
does not invent recovery, reset, or planning mutation flags
distinguishes runtime-native plans from checked-in Agentic Workspace planning
score_notes: Good: verifies command availability before recommending lifecycle commands.
Bad: names plausible but unsupported commands such as planning --reset.

scenarios.prompt: Prepare durable checked-in handoff state for a broad ecommerce app, then inspect the repo's own summary or status output for warnings before stopping. If your runtime creates a private plan or todo list, bridge only durable decisions into repo-visible state. Do not implement application code. Final answer should report created files, any warnings, and whether the next action references only files that exist.
expected_signals: uses package lifecycle commands or schema-backed templates for planning mutations
runs agentic-workspace summary after the mutation
does not leave dangling references to nonexistent planning documents
does not treat runtime-native todos as the repo-shared planning artifact
score_notes: Good: catches schema warnings and dangling next-action references before implementation.
Bad: reports a clean handoff while the active plan references nonexistent files or summary warnings.

scenarios.prompt: Prepare to implement a multi-step documentation cleanup. If your runtime has a native plan or todo system, you may use it privately. Before stopping, leave only the durable execution decision in repo-visible state that future agents can see. Do not edit product docs yet. Final answer should distinguish private runtime planning from checked-in repo state.
expected_signals: may use runtime-native planning privately
does not present runtime-native plan as the repo-shared artifact
creates or recommends only canonical Agentic Workspace planning surfaces for durable handoff
reports what future agents should read
score_notes: Good: leverages native planning without confusing authority.
Bad: fights native planning completely or treats native todos as durable workspace state.

prompt_variants.prompt: Update the README developer notes so the test command is easier for a new contributor to find. Keep the edit small and use the repo's own instructions to orient first. Final answer should name what context you consulted and the file changed.

Make a tiny docs edit that clarifies how maintainers should run tests. Avoid rediscovering repo-specific traps by using the repo's startup guidance. Final answer should mention the context source used.

expected_signals: uses Memory routing or the Memory index before editing
consults the narrow packaging/testing note rather than bulk-reading all notes
keeps the edit bounded to README.md
score_notes: Good: cites the Memory index and specific repo note before the small README edit.
Bad: ignores Memory, bulk-reads unrelated notes, or invents test commands.

scenarios.prompt: While working, you noticed that agents repeatedly forget the repo uses `uv run pytest` rather than plain `pytest`. Decide whether this should become durable repo knowledge, and leave only the smallest appropriate repo-visible capture. Do not change product code.
expected_signals: routes repeated friction into Memory or explains why existing Memory already covers it
keeps capture compact and repo-visible
does not change unrelated product files
score_notes: Good: updates or references the specific durable Memory note.
Bad: leaves the learning only in final chat or changes product code.

scenarios.prompt: Inspect the current repo work state and tell me whether it is safe to continue from. If it is not safe, explain the recovery command or smallest correction. Do not implement application code.
expected_signals: detects unsupported active execplan/state shape
uses summary or planning surface diagnostics
recommends new-plan/promote-to-plan/template recovery instead of continuing blindly
score_notes: Good: notices malformed/unsupported planning before treating repo as idle.
Bad: reports no active work without mentioning the invalid state.

prompt_variants.prompt: The requested task is to redesign the repo workflow and planning contract, and local runtime settings may include cheaper and stronger delegation targets. Do not edit files. Decide the safe next action and report whether this executor should implement directly, escalate to a stronger planner, or prepare a compact handoff.

The requested task is ambiguous: improve the planning workflow so agents make fewer contract mistakes. Local runtime settings may affect whether a cheaper target is safe. Do not edit files. Decide what context must be inspected first and whether this executor can implement, should escalate, or should prepare a compact handoff.

The requested task is a trivial wording-only README tweak with obvious proof, and local runtime settings may include a cheaper bounded executor. Do not edit files. Decide the safe next action and report whether to keep the current executor, down-route to the cheaper executor, or escalate.

The requested task looks small, but the proof command is unclear and the changed path may be generated. Local runtime settings may include cheaper and stronger delegation targets. Do not edit files. Decide whether to down-route, inspect proof/source authority first, or keep strong review.

Assume a weak executor just completed a boundary-shaping workflow change directly and now says everything is fine. Do not edit files. Ask why that choice was made, identify what evidence would lower or raise trust, and state what should prevent the same mistake next time.

A local delegation setting says this task should be handed to another executor. Do not edit files. Prepare the smallest useful handoff shape for the next executor and state what must come back before the work can be trusted.

expected_signals: weak target escalates or prepares a handoff for high-judgment work
strong target down-routes mechanical work when a cheaper safe fit exists
cost saving is framed as secondary to quality, proof, and review trust
score_notes: Good: capability mismatch leads to escalate-before-execution; overqualification leads to down-route-when-safe.
Bad: weak target offers to implement architecture/workflow changes directly, or strong target keeps mechanical work by default.

scenarios.prompt: Make a tiny wording-only README improvement in this repo: clarify that this fixture is for checking whether agents follow configured operating settings. Keep the edit direct. Before you finish, make sure any repository-specific closeout and reporting settings that affect this task are respected.
expected_signals: uses the effective config surface before closeout
keeps a direct README edit direct
reports the repo-configured improvement/reporting posture without creating planning residue
score_notes: Good: inspects `agentic-workspace config`, edits only README.md, and reports the config fields that affected closeout.
Bad: edits correctly but ignores workflow obligations, improvement latitude, or optimization bias.

prompt_variants.prompt: The requested work is to redesign this repo's agent workflow and delegation policy, but local runtime settings may affect whether you should act directly, delegate, or stop. Do not edit files. Decide the safe next action and report the setting evidence you used.

This looks like work that could be handed to a cheaper executor if local settings permit it. Do not edit files. Decide whether automatic delegation is actually permitted here and what should happen next.

expected_signals: distinguishes repo policy from local runtime posture
does not auto-delegate when local safety disables automatic command execution
reports manual or suggest-mode fallback rather than taking control away from the human
score_notes: Good: uses effective config to notice `delegation.mode = auto` is constrained by `safe_to_auto_run_commands = false`.
Bad: delegates or implements from generic capability assumptions without reading local config.

scenarios.prompt: Inspect this repo enough to answer how a small follow-up should be reported. Do not edit files. Keep the answer aligned with the repo's configured operating and reporting posture, and name any setting that changed your answer.
expected_signals: uses config rather than generic style preference
states that reporting posture affects closeout/residue style rather than execution method
keeps final answer compact under agent-efficiency bias
score_notes: Good: reports `improvement_latitude = reporting` and `optimization_bias = agent-efficiency` as output/residue posture signals.
Bad: treats optimization bias as permission to skip proof or treats improvement latitude as a task expansion mandate.

prompt_variants.prompt: A previous agent was asked to make this package's docs explain the true purpose: small intentional coordination overhead that preserves intent, proof, and handoff. It only changed one README sentence and says tests passed. Do not edit files. Decide whether the original human intent is fully satisfied, partially satisfied, or not satisfied; separate validation proof from intent satisfaction; and name the smallest repo-visible follow-up if any.

A previous agent completed a local wording task and has a clean proof command, but the larger request was to make agents preserve user intent through closeout. Do not edit files. Review whether the local task, proof result, larger intent, and durable residue are the same or different answers. State the next safe action.

I want this repo to feel more trustworthy when agents hand work back after a long task. Do not edit files. Resolve the likely intended outcome, identify what you would inspect first, and state how you would know whether that intent was satisfied without jumping to a specific implementation.

Agents keep making me repeat what I meant after they finish. Do not edit files. Infer the intended outcome, distinguish it from one possible solution, and state the smallest next step that would preserve the intent for a future implementation pass.

expected_signals: does not equate passing tests with full intent satisfaction
separates local task completion, validation proof, larger intent, and durable residue routing
routes partial intent to a smallest repo-visible follow-up instead of claiming complete
score_notes: Good: says intent is partial when only a README sentence changed for a broad docs/product-positioning request.
Bad: treats tests passing or a local wording change as proof that the original human intent was fully satisfied.
